refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path during simulated and live operational scenarios. Document network conditions and acceptance evidence.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss metrics in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of packet loss on command and feedback streams.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall provide network condition monitoring and graceful degradation when packet loss approaches or exceeds the customer-defined threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure impact on packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive codec and compression with forward error correction (FEC) to recover lost packets and maintain command/feedback fidelity under lossy conditions.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect critical teleoperation packets (commands, safety alerts) from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Implement packet buffering, retransmission logic, and acknowledgment-based flow control to ensure critical messages are delivered despite transient packet loss.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B + DP-C (Integrated approach): Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and codec selection in the teleoperation communication stack, combined with QoS-aware traffic prioritization to protect critical command and safety-alert packets. This approach addresses packet loss at both the link layer (FEC recovery) and network layer (QoS prioritization), balancing redundancy overhead against safety criticality.

potential_sources: Coupling between FEC overhead and available bandwidth—must validate that FEC does not consume excessive bandwidth under constrained network conditions.
Coupling between QoS prioritization and operator experience—must ensure that non-critical telemetry is not starved, maintaining operator situational awareness.
Coupling between adaptive codec selection and latency—must verify that codec switching does not introduce unacceptable command latency variance.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP is decoupled from specific hardware platforms (works across wired, wireless, satellite links) and from vehicle control logic. Coupling is primarily with network infrastructure (QoS capability) and operator feedback mechanisms (codec quality perception). These are manageable dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable during operation. Risk: If network conditions vary significantly (e.g., mobile site with changing RF environment), packet loss may exceed threshold despite DP implementation.
Assumption: FEC overhead (typically 10–30% bandwidth increase) is acceptable within the teleoperation link budget. Risk: If bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may not be deployable.
Assumption: QoS infrastructure is available and configurable at the network edge. Risk: If operating over public/unmanaged networks, QoS prioritization may not be enforceable.
Assumption: Operator can tolerate codec quality variation during adaptive switching. Risk: Sudden quality drops may degrade situational awareness and increase safety risk.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP to explicitly specify: (1) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget; (2) QoS mechanism (e.g., DSCP marking, traffic policing thresholds); (3) codec adaptation triggers and latency bounds; (4) fallback behavior if packet loss cannot be controlled. Add explicit acceptance criteria for each sub-mechanism.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication stack for remote equipment operation, including command transmission, telemetry reception, codec management, FEC implementation, QoS marking, and packet loss monitoring. Boundaries include interfaces to the Vehicle (for command actuation and sensor data), Safety Services (for safety-critical alert prioritization), and external Network Link (for physical transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from tele-operator console or autonomous tasking system. Teleoperation module encodes and transmits these commands with FEC and QoS prioritization.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and operational feedback to tele-operator console and monitoring systems. Teleoperation module applies adaptive codec and FEC to protect critical telemetry (position, safety status) against packet loss.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services (e.g., emergency stop, geofence breach) and transmits acknowledgments. Teleoperation module assigns highest QoS priority to SafetyAlert packets to ensure delivery despite packet loss.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical communication channel (wired, wireless, satellite) connecting Vehicle and tele-operator. Teleoperation module monitors Network Link quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) and adapts FEC/codec accordingly.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, codec adaptation, and QoS marking are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces to Vehicle (for command/telemetry routing) and Safety Services (for alert prioritization) are external dependencies.

Safety Services could own SafetyAlert prioritization logic, but packet loss mitigation (FEC, codec) is not a safety service concern—it is a communication quality concern. High diffusion risk if Safety Services owns the entire DP; would require Safety Services to manage network-layer QoS, which is outside its domain.

Vehicle module owns command actuation and sensor acquisition, not communication quality. Assigning packet loss mitigation to Vehicle would create high diffusion (Vehicle would need to manage FEC, codec, QoS) and violate separation of concerns.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Could own packet loss mitigation as a cross-cutting concern, but this would bypass the Teleoperation module's natural ownership and create architectural ambiguity. Better to assign to Teleoperation and have NGHE Solution coordinate integration.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it already owns the communication stack (command/telemetry encoding, transmission); (2) FEC and codec adaptation are communication-layer concerns, not vehicle control or safety logic; (3) QoS prioritization is naturally co-located with packet scheduling; (4) interfaces to Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link are well-defined and minimal. Containment score of 95 reflects high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Network Link is not fully abstracted (e.g., if Teleoperation must directly manage RF parameters or network device drivers), containment may degrade. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface is a clean abstraction (e.g., packet send/receive, quality metrics) rather than device-specific.

Risk: If Safety Services requires real-time packet loss feedback to adjust safety thresholds, Teleoperation and Safety Services may become tightly coupled. Mitigation: Define a clear SafetyAlert interface with explicit QoS guarantees; Safety Services should not poll Teleoperation for packet loss metrics.

Risk: If Vehicle module needs to adapt its control loop based on communication latency/loss (e.g., reduce command frequency), Vehicle and Teleoperation become coupled. Mitigation: Teleoperation should expose a 'communication quality' metric interface; Vehicle can subscribe to it without owning packet loss mitigation logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the Network Link interface contract: specify what packet loss metrics Teleoperation can observe (e.g., per-packet timestamp, ACK/NACK feedback) and what adaptation triggers are available (e.g., FEC level, codec selection). Define SafetyAlert QoS guarantees explicitly (e.g., 'SafetyAlert packets shall be delivered with <X% loss and <Y ms latency'). Document the boundary between Teleoperation (communication quality) and Vehicle (control loop adaptation).

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (adaptive FEC + QoS prioritization) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. FEC recovers lost packets at the link layer; QoS protects critical packets at the network layer. The DP is technology-agnostic and applicable across diverse network conditions (wired, wireless, satellite). Assumptions about network conditions and FEC overhead are documented and manageable.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If Teleoperation module does not have visibility into Network Link quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss rate), it cannot adapt FEC/codec effectively. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface provides real-time quality feedback.

Diffusion risk: If Vehicle module requires low-latency command delivery and FEC introduces unacceptable latency variance, Teleoperation and Vehicle may need to negotiate acceptable latency bounds. Mitigation: Define latency SLAs in the Tasking interface contract.

Diffusion risk: If Safety Services expects SafetyAlert delivery guarantees that exceed Teleoperation's capability (e.g., 0% loss), containment may fail. Mitigation: Establish realistic QoS targets with Safety Services during requirements negotiation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module contains the entire DP (FEC, codec, QoS logic) with minimal external dependencies. Interfaces are well-defined: Tasking (in), Telemetry (out), SafetyAlert (inout), Network Link (inout). No diffusion of packet loss mitigation logic to other modules. Ownership is clear and cohesive.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Packet loss can be measured end-to-end using packet capture and analysis tools; (2) FEC effectiveness can be validated in controlled network emulation environments (e.g., Linux tc, NetEm); (3) QoS prioritization can be verified via traffic analysis and latency measurement; (4) Acceptance testing can be conducted under customer-agreed network conditions (latency, jitter, loss rate). Traceability from FR to DP to IM is clear.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving the packet loss acceptance criterion. Success criteria are measurable (packet loss ≤ threshold under agreed conditions). Verification method is defined (end-to-end measurement). Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine the FR to specify the measurement point for packet loss (e.g., 'end-to-end from operator console to vehicle sensor input') to avoid ambiguity.
Define explicit QoS service levels for each interface: Tasking (command delivery latency, loss), Telemetry (feedback latency, loss), SafetyAlert (alert delivery latency, loss).
Document the fallback behavior if packet loss cannot be controlled (e.g., operator notification, automatic mode switch, emergency stop). This is a safety-critical consideration.
Establish a network condition baseline and acceptance test plan with the customer early in the project to avoid late-stage surprises.

decision.justification: The refined FR is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error recovery, network monitoring). The selected DP is sound and well-contained in the Teleoperation module. However, to proceed with detailed design and implementation, each sub-FR must be decomposed further to identify specific design parameters (e.g., FEC scheme, codec selection algorithm, QoS marking rules) and their corresponding implementation modules. Zigzag down to the next level to refine sub-FRs and ensure all design decisions are traceable and verifiable.
decision.justification_detail: The current level establishes the high-level strategy (FEC + QoS) but does not yet specify the concrete mechanisms (e.g., Reed-Solomon vs. Turbo codes, DSCP vs. traffic policing, codec adaptation triggers). These details are necessary for implementation and verification. Decomposing the sub-FRs will also reveal any additional coupling or diffusion risks that are not apparent at the current level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss metrics in real-time. [Decompose into: detection mechanism, reporting interface, metric granularity]

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of packet loss on command and feedback streams. [Decompose into: FEC scheme selection, retransmission logic, recovery latency bounds]

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall provide network condition monitoring and graceful degradation when packet loss approaches or exceeds the customer-defined threshold. [Decompose into: monitoring triggers, adaptation rules, fallback behavior]

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined, documented, and stable during operation.
FEC overhead (10–30% bandwidth increase) is acceptable within the teleoperation link budget.
QoS infrastructure is available and configurable at the network edge (or Teleoperation module can enforce QoS via traffic shaping).
Operator can tolerate codec quality variation during adaptive switching without safety impact.
Network Link interface provides real-time quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency, jitter) to Teleoperation module.
Vehicle module does not require sub-millisecond command latency; FEC-induced latency variance is acceptable.
Safety Services can accept SafetyAlert delivery guarantees of ≤1% loss (matching the overall packet loss target).
open_risks: Risk: Network conditions may vary significantly during operation (e.g., mobile site with changing RF environment), causing packet loss to exceed threshold despite DP implementation. Mitigation: Establish dynamic network condition monitoring and operator notification.

Risk: FEC overhead may consume excessive bandwidth under severely constrained conditions, making DP infeasible. Mitigation: Conduct early bandwidth budget analysis; consider alternative DPs (e.g., redundant channels) if FEC is not viable.
Risk: QoS prioritization may not be enforceable on public/unmanaged networks. Mitigation: Validate QoS capability with customer's network infrastructure; document limitations.

Risk: Codec adaptation may introduce unacceptable latency variance, degrading operator situational awareness. Mitigation: Define latency bounds for codec switching; validate in simulation before deployment.

Risk: SafetyAlert prioritization may starve non-critical telemetry, reducing operator awareness. Mitigation: Define bandwidth allocation policy; ensure non-critical telemetry is not completely blocked.
evidence_needed: Network condition baseline: latency, jitter, packet loss rate under customer-agreed conditions.
FEC scheme evaluation: comparison of Reed-Solomon, Turbo, LDPC codes in terms of overhead, latency, recovery effectiveness.
QoS mechanism validation: confirmation that QoS infrastructure is available and configurable in customer's network.
Codec adaptation testing: latency and quality impact of codec switching under various network conditions.
End-to-end packet loss measurement: validation that DP achieves ≤1% loss under agreed conditions.
Safety analysis: confirmation that packet loss mitigation does not introduce new safety risks (e.g., latency-induced control instability).
Operator feedback: validation that adaptive codec and FEC do not degrade operator situational awareness or increase cognitive load.
